summary: Storm‑charged second set with legendary Wharf Rat and soaring Sugar Magnolia
blurb: The band fed off the thunderstorm, delivering a powerhouse second set highlighted by an epic Wharf Rat and a soaring Sugar Magnolia jam. Jerry’s guitar and Phil’s bass lock into the lightning‑lit grooves while Brent adds storm‑like keyboard flourishes. The performance is widely regarded as one of 1983’s most memorable rain‑soaked shows.
review: June 20, 1983 at Merriweather Post Pavilion turned into a meteorological masterpiece that the Dead translated into pure musical electricity. The first set laid down a solid groove with tight renditions of “The Music Never Stopped” and “Hell In A Bucket,” but it was the second set that truly captured the night’s fury. As thunder rolled, Jerry Garcia unleashed a ferocious lead on “Wharf Rat,” his tone described by listeners as “god talking to Jerry,” while Phil Lesh dropped bass bombs that seemed to challenge the storm itself. The sequence from “Truckin'” through “The Other One” built into a marathon of improvisation, climaxing in an unforgettable, rain‑swept “Sugar Magnolia” where Bob Weir’s wild “Bob Star” rap and Brent Mydland’s storm‑like keyboard solo on “Little Red Rooster” lit the night. The encore, a heartfelt “It’s All Over Now Baby Blue,” provided a quiet resolution after the chaos. Across multiple audience recordings—most notably gd83-06-20.fob-senn.sws.11931.sbeok.shnf—the show shines despite occasional tinny drum channels, offering one of the most vivid examples of the Dead feeding off nature’s own rhythm.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Seven of eight recordings are marked positive, using language like 'legendary,' 'epic,' and 'unstoppable.' The lone mixed review still notes strong moments without significant criticism. No negative consensus appears, so a strong positive adjustment is justified.

best_recording.reason: Surprisingly clear audience tape that captures the storm ambience and key moments (Wharf Rat, Sugar Magnolia) with enough fidelity to feel near‑studio despite some tinny drum sections.

key_highlights: Thunder‑driven second set that feels like a live weather report
Epic “Wharf Rat” jam synced with lightning strikes
Soaring, extended “Sugar Magnolia” featuring Bob’s rap and Brent’s stormy keyboard solo
Brent Mydland’s ferocious fills and the unforgettable Rooster (Little Red Rooster) solo

band_performance.Jerry: Powerful, lightning‑charged guitar work throughout, especially on "Wharf Rat" and the soaring "Sugar Magnolia"; a few note a botched bridge in "China Cat Sunflower" but overall dominant.
band_performance.Phil: Bomb‑dropping bass that rode the thunder, with massive low‑end on "The Other One" and "Wharf Rat," challenging the storm gods throughout the set.
band_performance.Bob: Wild vocal rap (“Bob Star”) and intense guitar on "Sugar Magnolia," plus a fierce spoken rap during the jam; his attempt to smash his guitar was over‑the‑top but added to the chaos.
band_performance.Brent: Ferocious keyboard fills that stepped forward, highlighted by a storm‑like solo on "Little Red Rooster" (referred to as “Rooster”) and overall driving the second‑set intensity.
band_performance.Mickey: Relentless drumming that drove the stormy energy, matching the thunder with relentless rhythm.
band_performance.Billy: Equally relentless, providing a powerful dual‑drum foundation that kept the momentum charging forward.
